title: Data-Oriented Design
rules: shape data around the real access pattern
separate logical identity from dense execution location when needed
prefer contiguous traversal for hot paths
split hot and cold state when the workload justifies it
keep staging, invalidation, and ownership boundaries explicit
procedure: Identify the workload, hot fields, and traversal order.
Classify data as hot, cold, identity, derived, scratch, or published output.
Choose packed, staged, split, indexed, or sparse representation based on access pattern.
Define update, invalidation, and recompute boundaries.
Validate traversal cost, cache behavior, and ownership clarity.

primary_when: request is about data layout, cache-friendly traversal, batch processing, or throughput-oriented structure design
request is about minimizing pointer chasing or separating hot and cold data
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about plugin bootstrap, IMGUI, or assembly metadata parsing

solves: mapping workload shape to data layout
reducing overhead from object-centric decomposition on hot paths
making traversal, invalidation, and batching predictable
does_not_solve: algorithmic complexity problems unrelated to layout or traversal
user-facing architecture concerns that only incidentally affect performance

core_principles: Shape data around access patterns, not around conceptual object boundaries.
Prefer linear, predictable traversal over scattered pointer-heavy access.
Keep hot data compact and physically close to the code paths that consume it.
Separate hot and cold state so infrequent data does not bloat hot loops.
Use explicit batch boundaries and staged transforms when they make cost predictable.
constraints: Do not add abstraction layers that hide actual traversal cost.
Do not introduce data transformations that cost more than the layout win they claim to provide.
Keep ownership and update boundaries explicit so layout decisions remain inspectable.
Validate proposed layouts against real access patterns rather than intuition alone.
decision_rules: If the same data is processed in long linear loops, prefer contiguous storage and index-based traversal.
If most fields are rarely accessed, split cold state away from the hot path.
If a system spends time chasing references across heterogeneous objects, flatten or stage the data flow.
If batching can turn many tiny updates into one predictable pass, prefer the staged pass.
If the workload is sparse and irregular, design explicit indirection boundaries instead of pretending it is dense.

preferred_patterns: struct-of-arrays or hybrid layouts shaped by traversal needs
explicit stage passes over stable buffers
versioned or dirty-marked data blocks when recomputation must be selective
batch-oriented transforms between cold control data and hot execution data
avoid_patterns: object graphs on hot paths with deep pointer chasing
per-element virtual dispatch inside tight loops
layout choices based on conceptual neatness rather than measured access patterns
whole-system scans when dirty subsets can be isolated

recipes.name: Hot/Cold Split
recipes.when: A frequently executed loop touches only a subset of each record's fields.
steps: Identify the fields read or written in the hot loop.
Move infrequently accessed state into a separate cold structure.
Keep stable identifiers or indices to reconnect cold state only when needed.
Re-measure cache behavior and traversal time after the split.

recipes.name: Batch-Oriented Execution Buffer
recipes.when: Feature code emits many fine-grained updates that can be processed together.
steps: Collect source changes into an explicit buffer or queue.
Transform the buffered data into a linear execution representation.
Run one predictable pass over the execution buffer.
Apply results back through explicit ownership boundaries.

pitfalls: Calling anything data-oriented without defining the actual traversal pattern.
Flattening everything indiscriminately, including cold or irregular state.
Creating conversion passes that erase the performance gained from the new layout.
Confusing data-oriented layout with mandatory multithreading.
review_checklist: Is the layout shaped by measured access patterns rather than abstract modeling?
Are hot and cold fields separated where the workload justifies it?
Does the design reduce pointer chasing on the real hot path?
Are batch and traversal boundaries explicit and inspectable?
Is recomputation scoped to the data that actually changed?

references.title: Data-Oriented Design
references.interpretation: Use for layout heuristics, stage design, and anti-pattern review.

references.title: Performance Domain Foundations
references.interpretation: Use for the broader cost model and measurement discipline around layout decisions.
